There are two main ways of organizing strings, translations. In a project, you can either decide
a reference language. Not surprisingly, most of the time it's English. And every word that is shown to
the users is directly written in that reference language in the source code. And then internationalization
libraries, and check out if there are any translations for those words in a translation file, and swap those.
But you have also another strategy that is the hierarchical identifiers. You got it.
And basically, you treat all the words shown on the screen as external data that you reference using
symbolic keys like those. The benefit of this approach is that those identifiers are clear for translators,
because we have context. And you can edit the text shown to users even in your own language.
So if you want to change what we also call microcopy, the word that you use for the users,
even in English, you can do so without changing the source code, which is something kind of nice.
And it's also cool because in a thinking way, English is just another language. It's not the reference
language. So that's a cool way to do that. But blue sky shows the former. And honestly,
I do understand that. Because let's be honest, most English speaking developers just want to iterate

and tricks to make sure that we stop excluding women from the language. So, here are two main
strategies that we can use to implement écriture inclusive. Either write both masculine and feminine

And to do that, you use some points médians, which are basically interpreted, but nobody
